draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Runners want real-time pace feedback with fluid animations rather than static number updates
facade.label: Swipe to see pace
facade.acceptImplies: Weather app should use continuous animated transitions for speed/pace metrics, micro-interactions on data changes, smooth gauge animations
facade.rejectImplies: Weather app should favor instant static displays, grid-based layouts, minimal motion for core running data

evidence.hypothesis: Runners want real-time pace feedback with fluid animations rather than static number updates
evidence.implication: Weather app should use continuous animated transitions for speed/pace metrics, micro-interactions on data changes, smooth gauge animations

facade.hypothesis: Runners prioritize glanceable numbers (pace, temp, humidity) over narrative detail; a predominantly numeric interface with supporting context reduces cognitive load during activity
facade.label: Giant metrics, minimal text
facade.acceptImplies: User values speed of comprehension; design should feature large, bold data points with brief labels; text explanations are secondary
facade.rejectImplies: User wants context and story; plain numbers lack meaning without surrounding detail; design should embed metrics within descriptive prose or explanations

facade.hypothesis: Weather runners prefer clean, high-contrast layouts over dense information clusters
facade.label: Bright minimal cards
facade.acceptImplies: User values clarity and fast scanning—likely prefers large readable text, generous spacing, and card-based organization for quick glance data (temp, pace, distance)
facade.rejectImplies: User may want richer visual density, darker aesthetics, or more atmospheric/immersive design—possibly prefers a unified dashboard view or moodier color palette that feels more premium or integrated

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners performs better with warm, inviting interface colors and soft lighting effects that feel encouraging rather than clinical—reducing cognitive load during pre-run decisions.
facade.label: Warm glow
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into amber, gold, and peachy tones; soft shadows; approachable typography; maybe a sunrise metaphor in the visual language.
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward cool blues, silvers, clean whites, sharp contrast, and a more neutral or tech-forward aesthetic.

facade.hypothesis: A runner-focused weather app should sound upbeat, motivational, and conversational—matching the energy of active users—rather than adopting a neutral, corporate tone.
facade.label: Energetic voice
facade.acceptImplies: Embrace warm, encouraging language in notifications, copy, and UI. Use exclamation points, motivational phrasing ('You've got this!'), and personality in error states. Treat the app as a running buddy.
facade.rejectImplies: Adopt a calm, measured, formal tone. Use neutral language ('Conditions unfavorable'), technical accuracy over encouragement, and minimal personality. Treat the app as a factual instrument.

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from persistent navigation (sidebar) combined with quick-switch tabs for key views (current conditions, route forecast, history).
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation structure — persistent sidebar vs. tab-based switching
facade.label: Sidebar with tabs
facade.acceptImplies: User values easy context-switching between weather layers without losing place; likely prefers desktop-first or tablet-friendly layouts.
facade.rejectImplies: User prioritizes mobile-first, single-focus views; likely wants full-screen map or run details without divided attention.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance widgets on home screen vs. navigating into detailed weather views
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. straightforward, factual reporting
Whether user wants calm, reassuring interface vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather data
Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, immediate feedback when checking conditions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance widgets on home screen vs. navigating into detailed weather views
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. straightforward, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, reassuring interface vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, immediate feedback when checking conditions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer discrete, snappy number changes for wind speed over smooth animated transitions, wanting immediate clarity over fluid motion
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — continuous animation vs. instant updates
facade.label: Wind speed jumps
facade.acceptImplies: Weather app should use snappy, instantaneous value changes with sharp transitions for wind/conditions data; runners value quick legibility over fluid aesthetics
facade.rejectImplies: Confirms runners prefer smooth animated transitions for all metric updates; continuous motion language should dominate the weather display design

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
